6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Project risk management typically collapses a crucial distinction: output risk concerns the delivery of the tangible thing the project produces (the system, feature, building, or integration), which is highly legible and controllable within project boundaries through metrics like schedule, budget, scope, and technical quality. In contrast, outcome risk concerns the real-world change that output was meant to create — adoption, revenue, behavior shift, or strategic advantage — which materializes later, outside the project team’s control, amid market and organizational variables, often with no remaining owner after handover. Conventional approaches fill risk registers with well-managed output risks while leaving an outcome-shaped hole, treating the Iron Triangle as an unfalsifiable proxy for success that invites Goodhart’s Law distortions. A better architecture requires falsifiable outcome hypotheses, leading indicators tracked before closure, persistent ownership, differentiated governance for necessary (value-creating) versus negligent or reckless risks, and real-option decision points — structural changes that current AI-PMO tools cannot easily address since they optimize only the visible, internal output data.
Start with a distinction the field usually collapses: an output is the thing the project delivers — the system, the building, the feature, the merger integration. An outcome is the change in the world that output was supposed to cause — adoption, revenue, capability, behaviour change, strategic position. Most project risk management is rigorously output-oriented and only nominally outcome-oriented, and this isn’t an oversight — it’s structurally determined by what’s legible.
Output risk is legible because it lives inside the project’s own boundaries: schedule slippage, budget overrun, scope creep, technical defects, resource unavailability, vendor failure. All of these can be tracked with data the project team controls, attributed to causes within the project timeframe, and closed out at go-live. Outcome risk is almost the opposite on every dimension: it materialises after the project team has disbanded, it’s entangled with market conditions and organisational behaviour the project never controlled, and there’s frequently no single owner left once the PM has moved to the next initiative and the operational team that inherited the output never agreed to the original success criteria.
So the typical risk register is a catalogue of output risk with an outcome risk shaped hole in it. “Integration risk,” “key person dependency,” “vendor delivery risk,” “technical debt” — all present and properly managed. “This may not be adopted,” “the business case assumption may not hold,” “the market may have moved by delivery,” “the organisation may not change its behaviour even with the new tool in place” — almost never present as managed risks, because nobody owns them and no governance gate asks the question.
The Iron Triangle as an Unfalsifiable Proxy
Time, cost, and scope are useful because they’re measurable in real time. The trouble is what happens once they become the definition of success rather than a proxy for it. This is a Goodhart’s Law problem dressed up as project discipline: once “on time, on budget, in scope” becomes the target that gets reported, escalated, and rewarded, the project optimises for hitting that target — sometimes by visibly trading away the very thing the project existed to produce. Cutting scope to protect the date. Cutting quality to protect the budget. Shipping the output and declaring success while the actual hypothesis — will this thing produce value — is never tested, let alone falsified.
This is the project-level version of the governance closure discussed earlier: a system that reports green/amber/red on output metrics has built itself an unfalsifiable proxy for success. It can be perfectly correct about the proxy and never discover whether the underlying bet paid off, because the project closes before that question becomes answerable, and nobody downstream is mandated to come back and check.
Three Kinds of Risk, Not One
Most risk management treats risk as a single dimension to be minimised. A more useful frame separates risk into three categories that get systematically conflated:
Negligent risk-avoidance — eliminating output risk by shrinking ambition until the project is safe but nearly valueless. A “transformation programme” that becomes a UI refresh. A genuinely novel capability that gets descoped into a feature nobody asked for, purely because the novel version had an honest risk profile that made governance uncomfortable. This is risk minimisation that destroys outcome potential while looking disciplined on every output metric.
Necessary risk — the irreducible uncertainty inherent in pursuing something whose value genuinely isn’t known yet. If you’re building something where the outcome is uncertain by nature — will customers adopt this, will this market exist in eighteen months, will this organisational change actually take — there is no risk-free version of that bet. Eliminating the risk eliminates the bet. This is the risk a portfolio needs some deliberate allocation toward, and it requires a different governance posture: amber status here isn’t a warning sign, it’s the expected signature of a project worth doing.
Reckless risk — risk taken with no feedback loop, no kill criteria, no informational basis for the bet — gambling wearing the costume of boldness. The dangerous thing is that reckless risk and necessary risk look identical on a dashboard, because both report as uncertain. The only thing that distinguishes them is whether there’s a falsifiable hypothesis underneath, with defined signals that would tell you early if you’re wrong. Without that, “necessary risk” is just a flattering name for reckless risk after the fact, and “this was worth the risk” is unfalsifiable in exactly the way good epistemics forbids.
Most PMO governance pushes everything toward negligent risk-avoidance, because that’s what’s measurable and defensible at a stage gate. The handful of projects that don’t get pushed there often slide to reckless instead, precisely because nobody built the outcome-tracking infrastructure that would let necessary risk be managed as necessary risk rather than waved through on conviction.
What a Risk Architecture for Outcomes Would Require
If success-as-outcome were genuinely the target, the architecture looks different from typical PMO practice in specific, checkable ways:
An explicit, falsifiable outcome hypothesis at initiation. Not “improve customer experience” but “if this works, conversion on this flow increases by X within Y weeks of launch, and if it doesn’t move within that window, the hypothesis was wrong.” A hypothesis that can’t specify what would prove it false isn’t a hypothesis, it’s a mission statement, and missions can’t be risk-managed.
Leading outcome indicators tracked before project closure, not after. This requires a feedback loop into the live environment — pilots, staged rollouts, instrumented betas — that produces outcome signal while the project still has budget and authority to course-correct. Most projects are scoped to end at deployment, which means the only point where outcome data exists is also the point where nobody’s left to act on it.
Ownership that survives the project structure. Outcome accountability has to outlive the PM and sponsor roles, which means either the operational receiving team co-owns the success criteria from day one, or the project doesn’t actually close until the outcome hypothesis has been tested, not just until the output has been delivered.
A risk appetite statement that separates the three categories above. Necessary risk gets a different governance treatment than output risk — different reporting cadence, different escalation triggers, explicit tolerance for sustained amber as long as the leading indicators are being tracked and the kill criteria are pre-agreed.
Decision points built as real options, not sunk-cost checkpoints. Predefined pivot and kill criteria tied to outcome signals, agreed before the emotional and political investment in the project makes that conversation impossible to have honestly.
Why This Is Especially Hard for AI-in-PMO Tools
This connects directly to the earlier point about AI in PMO SaaS. Every plausible AI feature in the current generation of tooling — predictive RAG status, AI-generated risk entries, AI estimate suggestions — operates entirely on output risk. They’re built on schedule data, financial actuals, and status text, all of which lives inside the project boundary and is available before go-live. There’s no equivalent AI feature pitched for outcome risk, and it’s not because the technology can’t do it — it’s because the data doesn’t exist in the same system. Usage telemetry, adoption curves, and market signal live in product analytics or operational systems owned by a different team, on a different timeline, frequently not integrated with the PMO tool at all.
So an AI layer applied to current PMO data will make output-risk management more efficient and outcome-risk management exactly as invisible as it already is — possibly more invisible, because a more sophisticated-looking tool creates more confidence that risk is being managed, when only one of the two relevant risks actually is. The precondition for AI to help with outcome risk isn’t a better model. It’s an organisational decision to keep projects open, accountable, and instrumented past the point where the output gets handed over — which is a governance redesign, not a feature request, and exactly the kind of unglamorous structural fix that doesn’t ship in a quarter and doesn’t win a demo.
